This document investigates how substitution elasticities affect the aggregation of sectoral productivity shocks within an economy's production network. The excerpt discusses the limitations of linear aggregation methods, particularly when moving beyond the standard Cobb-Douglas technology, and highlights that non-Cobb-Douglas structures can lead to nonlinear Domar aggregation, potentially causing network transformations and singularities.\n\nproductivity growths can be mapped into national aggregate output growth by linear aggregation using Domar weights. Linear aggregation of
sectoral productivity growths, however, is only possible in an economy where the representative household and all the production sectors aggregate commodities in a Cobb-Douglas
fashion, so that the monetary input-output coefficients remain constant. [2] Accordingly, Acemoglu et al (2012, 2017); Acemoglu and Azar (2020) studied Domar
aggregation under Cobb-Douglas technology where Domar weights are the product of representative household’s expenditure shares and the (monetary) Leontief inverse. In this case,
the volatility of the aggregate output fluctuations will contract disproportionally to the number of sectors whose productivity shocks are being aggregated, owing to the granular property
(Gabaix, 2011) of the Leontief inverse. Baqaee and Farhi (2019); Nakano and Nishimura
(2021) then studied aggregate fluctuations of an economy comprising non-Cobb-Douglas
technology and showed that aggregate fluctuations will have nonnormal tails even if sectoral
productivity shocks have normal tails, owing to the nonlinear nature of Domar aggregation
that can potentially induce network transformation.
From empirical perspective, Kim et al (2017); Nakano and Nishimura (2024) examined
economy-wide propagation of innovation, where innovation is proxied by productivity shocks
and their propagation is concerned across the production network of sectors with empirically
estimated CES (constant elasticity of substitution) unit cost functions. In this setting, the fixed
point solution to the general equilibrium system of equations is solved by way of recursive
mapping from factor prices onto commodity unit costs which will globally converge to a
unique equilibrium, owing to the concavity of the mapping, if only the solution existed in
the positive-finite domain. During the course of analyses, however, there were cases (of productivity shocks) where the recursive mapping experienced nonconvergence, indicating the
absence of the solution in the positive-finite domain.
The abovementioned nonlinear Domar aggregation (which thus implies synergism among
productivity shocks) and potential zero/infinite prices (which thus implies singularity of the

This document investigates how substitution elasticities affect the aggregation of production networks, particularly focusing on nonlinear Domar aggregation. The excerpt explains that because Cobb-Douglas technology yields stable input-output linkages, the study instead examines CES production networks with a universal substitution elasticity $\sigma$. This setting allows the authors to use linear algebra to handle general equilibrium solutions while keeping the final commodity price assessment simple and Cobb-Douglas.\n\n3

input-output linkages) can never be encountered under production networks base on CobbDouglas technology, where the monetary input-output linkages are stable. In this study we
hence explore the role of substitution elasticities in regard to the emergence of network singularity and synergism in nonlinear Domar aggregation. [3] In so doing, we work on CES production networks with a universal substitution elasticity σ, where the system of unit cost functions becomes linear with respect to the transcendent variables, i.e., variables transformed by
the power of (1 - σ)th order. The final assessment of commodity prices by the representative
household, nontheless, remains as Cobb-Douglas, for simplicity.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In the follwing section we present the benchmark general equilibrium system of equations encompassing multifactor CES unit cost functions with single (universal) substitution elasticity, and derive the Domar aggregation function with respect to the productivity shocks. The universal substitution elasticity setting with
the use of transcendent variables allows us to handle general equilibrium solutions by means
of linear algebra. In Section 3, we discuss network singularity and specify the conditions
under which such situation can actually take place. We find that an inelastic (elastic) network may encounter a negative (positive) singularity. In Section 4, we discuss synergy in the
economic evaluation of two different innovations. We find that an inelastic (elastic) network
entails negative (positive) synergism. Section 5 concludes the paper.

2.3 Network transformation

In this study, the monetary input-output coefficient matrix is reffered to as the production
network. A sector’s monetary input-output coefficient for the ith factor is its cost-share of
the ith factor. By applying Shephard’s lemma to CES unit cost function of the jth sector, and
together with zero profit condition, cost-share for the ith factor, which we denote by sij, can
be evaluated as follows:

------------------------------------------------------------------------
This document investigates how substitution elasticity affects the stability of the economy-wide production network when productivity shocks occur. The excerpt details the calculation of monetary input-output coefficients, or cost-shares, for a sector using the CES unit cost function. It explains that these cost-shares only remain constant despite productivity shocks if the elasticity of substitution is neutral ($\sigma=1$), otherwise, the network structure changes.\n\nmatrix is reffered to as the production
network. A sector’s monetary input-output coefficient for the ith factor is its cost-share of
the ith factor. By applying Shephard’s lemma to CES unit cost function of the jth sector, and
together with zero profit condition, cost-share for the ith factor, which we denote by sij, can
be evaluated as follows:

�1−σ
= αijζj [π][i]

πj

sij = αij

pi
zjpj Here, πi = (pi) [1][−][σ] and πj = (pj) [1][−][σ] are solutions to the general equilibrium system of
equations (7) that must only depend on the sectoral productivity levels z = (z1, · · ·, zn).
We know, by virtue of (8), that sij = αij at the reference state, since it is 1 = z = ζ
and hence, π = p = 1. Conversely, productivity shocks, i.e., z = 1 will transform the
production network, i.e., sij = αij, only if the elasticity of substitution is nonneutral, i.e.,
σ = 1. Note, if the elasticity of substitution is neutral, i.e., σ = 1, it is always the case that
sij = αij, that is, the production network is invariable regardless of the productivity shocks
and associated changes in prices.

This document investigates the role of substitution elasticity in understanding how sectoral productivity shocks aggregate across an economy's production network. The excerpt specifically details how the method of Domar aggregation, which maps sectoral productivity shocks to aggregate growth, changes depending on whether the substitution elasticity ($\sigma$) is neutral ($\sigma=1$), inelastic ($\sigma<1$), or elastic ($\sigma>1$). It provides the mathematical formulas for the resulting aggregate growth ($\log V$) under these different elasticity conditions.\n\n6 Satoshi Nakano, Kazuhiko Nishimura

For later convenience, let us call a production network subject to substitution elasticity less than unity (σ < 1), an _inelastic_ _network_, and that subject to substitution elasticity
greater than unity (σ - 1), an _elastic network_ . A _neutroelastic network_ (σ = 1) is otherwise
called a Cobb-Douglas production network. Both inelastic and elastic networks are subject
to transformation given sectoral productivity shocks, whereas neutroelastic network is not.

2.4 Domar aggregation

As mentioned earlier, Domar aggregation is a function that maps the set of sectoral productivity shocks (in terms of growths), i.e., log z = (log z1, · · ·, log zn), onto the aggregate
economic growth, i.e., log V . If all sectoral productions universally entail neutral substitution
elasticity, i.e., σ = 1, the Domar aggregation (under Cobb-Douglas utility) can be described,
by virtue of equations (2) and (11), as follows:

log V = (log z) [I − A] [−][1] κ (σ = 1) (12)

Otherwise, if all sectoral productions universally entail a single nonneutral substitution elasticity, i.e., σ = 1, the Domar aggregation (under Cobb-Douglas utility) can be described, by
virtue of equations (2) and (8), as follows:

1 ��     - �−1 �
log V = I − e [(][σ][−][1) log][ z][�] A e [(][σ][−][1) log][ z][�] α0
σ − 1 [log]

κ (σ = 1) (13)

This document investigates how the substitution elasticity of sectoral production affects the aggregation of production networks, particularly concerning the emergence of nonlinearity in Domar aggregation. The excerpt discusses the mathematical description of Domar aggregation under different elasticity conditions, contrasting the linear case ($\sigma=1$) with the nonlinear case when the elasticity is nonneutral. This highlights that the linearity or nonlinearity of the aggregation, and the resulting network transformation, depends critically on whether the substitution elasticity is neutral or not.\n\nV = (log z) [I − A] [−][1] κ (σ = 1) (12)

Otherwise, if all sectoral productions universally entail a single nonneutral substitution elasticity, i.e., σ = 1, the Domar aggregation (under Cobb-Douglas utility) can be described, by
virtue of equations (2) and (8), as follows:

1 ��     - �−1 �
log V = I − e [(][σ][−][1) log][ z][�] A e [(][σ][−][1) log][ z][�] α0
σ − 1 [log]

κ (σ = 1) (13) Formula (12) indicates that Domar aggregation is linear as long as the elasticity of substitution across the production network is neutral. Note that the column vector [I − A] [−][1] κ
corresponds to the Domar weights. The linearity stems from the robustness of the production
network against sectoral productivity shocks under neutral substitution elasticity. In contrast,
formula (13) indicates that Domar aggregation is nonlinear if the elasticity of substitution
across the production network is nonneutral. The nonlinearity stems from the fragility of
the production network against sectoral productivity shocks under nonneutral substitution
elasticity. Fallacy of composition (or, synergism) will never take place for linear Domar aggregation that does not evoke network transformation, whereas it will take place for nonlinear
Domar aggregation that brings about network transfromation.

3 Singularities

3.1 Two-sector model

Consider a two-sector general equilibrium system where commodity prices are determined
by CES unit cost functions with universal substitution elasticity σ, as follows:

(p1) [1][−][σ] = (z1) [σ][−][1][ �] α01 + α21(p2) [1][−][σ][�]

(14)

With (18) and (19) combined, we see that, 1) some productivity decline (z ≤ 1) under
inelastic network (σ < 1) can create a situation where an immense amount of labor is needed
to offset other factors of production (m0 →∞), and 2) some productivity increase (z ≥ 1)
under elastic network (σ - 1) can create a situation where only the smallest amount of
labor is needed to offset other factors of production (m0 → 0). We hereafter refer to 1) as
negative singularity and 2) as positive singularity. It must, however, be important to note that
these singularities stem from the unlimited possibility of factor substitution installed in the
isoquant of CES production function.

This document discusses the role of substitution elasticity in analyzing production networks, particularly in the context of Domar aggregation. The excerpt details the mathematical conditions for the viability of a "transcendent network" $\langle\zeta\rangle A$, which is crucial for solving the system of unit cost functions derived from the production network. Specifically, it establishes criteria based on the eigenvalues of the network matrix $A$ to determine when the network is row viable or when its viability is violated.\n\n9

Definition (Column viability) . A network, i.e., n × n matrix W, is said to be column viable,
if for any column vector f - 0, there exists a column vector y - 0 such that y = f + Wy.

Definition (Row viability) . A network, i.e., n × n matrix W, is said to be row viable, if for
any row vector v - 0, there exists a row vector q > 0 such that q = v + qW.

Assumption. The reference network A is nondefective, meaning all n nonzero eigenvalues
are distinct, thus diagonalizable.

By virtue of Lemmas 3, 4, 5, and 6, which we append to Appendix D with proofs, we
know that reference network A is row (and also column) viable and that A [∞] = 0. Moreover,
by virtue of the above Assumption, A is diagonalizable as follows:

A = Q ⟨λ⟩ Q [−][1]              - 0 (20)

where λ = (λ1, · · ·, λn) denotes the set of n nonzero eigenvalues and Q is some invertable
matrix. Since A [∞] = Q ⟨λ⟩ [∞] Q [−][1], and hence, ⟨λ⟩ [∞] = Q [−][1] A [∞] Q = 0, we know that

|λi| < 1 i = 1, · · ·, n (21)

By Lemma 6, we know the transcendent network ⟨ζ⟩ A will be row viable, i.e., for any
row vector α0 ⟨ζ⟩ - 0, there exists a row vector π - 0 such that π = α0 ⟨ζ⟩ + π ⟨ζ⟩ A, or
the solution to equation (7) exists, while [I −⟨ζ⟩ A] is invertable, as long as (⟨ζ⟩ A) [∞] = 0.
By virtue of (20), the transcendent network can be evaluated as follows:

⟨ζ⟩ A = ⟨ζ⟩ Q ⟨λ⟩ Q [−][1] < ζmaxQ ⟨λ⟩ Q [−][1] = Q ⟨ζmaxλ⟩ Q [−][1]

                 - ζminQ ⟨λ⟩ Q [−][1] = Q ⟨ζ _min_ λ⟩ Q [−][1]

where ζ _max_ = argmax(ζ1, · · ·, ζn) and ζ _min_ = argmin(ζ1, · · ·, ζn). Therefore,

⟨ζ _max_ λ⟩ [∞]           - Q [−][1] (⟨ζ⟩ A) [∞] Q > ⟨ζ _min_ λ⟩ [∞]           - 0 (22)

By virtue of (21) and (22) we arrive at the following lemmas:

Lemma 1 _Row viability of_ ⟨ζ⟩ A _is secured if_ ζ _max_ < 1/ |λi| > 1 _for all_ i = 1, · · ·, n _._

Lemma 2 _Row viability of_ ⟨ζ⟩ A _is violated if_ ζ _min_ - 1/ |λi| > 1 _for all_ i = 1, · · ·, n _._

Note that violation of row viability (for a network such that W [∞] = ∞) implies:

This document investigates how substitution elasticity affects the stability and aggregation of production networks, particularly when subjected to productivity shocks. The excerpt details the mathematical consequences of this analysis, presenting lemmas and propositions that characterize when commodity prices become infinite or zero based on the network's elasticity ($\sigma$). Specifically, it summarizes the conditions for "negative singularity" in inelastic networks and "positive singularity" in elastic networks.\n\nA) [∞] Q > ⟨ζ _min_ λ⟩ [∞]           - 0 (22)

By virtue of (21) and (22) we arrive at the following lemmas:

Lemma 1 _Row viability of_ ⟨ζ⟩ A _is secured if_ ζ _max_ < 1/ |λi| > 1 _for all_ i = 1, · · ·, n _._

Lemma 2 _Row viability of_ ⟨ζ⟩ A _is violated if_ ζ _min_ - 1/ |λi| > 1 _for all_ i = 1, · · ·, n _._

Note that violation of row viability (for a network such that W [∞] = ∞) implies: q = v + qW = v I + W + W [2] + · · · + W [∞][�] →∞

Thus, violation of row viability for ⟨ζ⟩ A implies π = p [1][−][σ] →∞, or transcendent network
singularity, and by Lemma 2, this occurs if ζ _min_ = zˇ [σ][−][1] - 1, but by Lemma 1, this will
never occur if ζ _max_ = zˆ [σ][−][1] < 1. Note that zˇ < 1 and zˆ - 1 if σ < 1, whereas zˇ - 1 and
zˆ < 1 if σ - 1. Moreover, p [1][−][σ] = ∞ implies p →∞ if σ < 1, whereas p → 0 if σ - 1.
We now can summarize the results obtained thus far below:

Proposition 1 (Negative singularity) . _Under an inelastic network (_ σ < 1 _), any productivity_
_increase (_ z - 1 _) will lead to finite commodity price (_ p ≪∞ _), whereas some productivity_
_decrease (_ z < 1 _) can lead to infinite commodity price (_ p →∞ _)._

Proposition 2 (Positive singularity) . _Under_ _an_ _elastic_ _network_ _(_ σ - 1 _),_ _any_ _productivity_
_decrease (_ z < 1 _) will lead to finite commodity price (_ p ≪∞ _), whereas some productivity_
_increase (_ z - 1 _) can lead to zero commodity price (_ p → 0 _)._

This document investigates how substitution elasticity affects the aggregation of sectoral production in an economy-wide network. The excerpt focuses on a two-sector model, deriving explicit formulas for the equilibrium prices ($\pi_1, \pi_2$) under productivity shocks ($\delta, \epsilon$). These derived equations demonstrate how the interaction between productivity changes in different sectors leads to specific multiplicative relationships, illustrating the concept of synergism.\n\n10 Satoshi Nakano, Kazuhiko Nishimura

4 Synergism

4.1 Two-sector model

Consider the previous two-sector model (14) redisplayed below in transcendent mode:

π1 = δ (α01 + α21π2)

(23)
π2 = ε (α02 + α12π1)

where we use (δ, ε) instead of (ζ1, ζ2) for convenience. Hereafter, we denote the equilibrium solution as a function of exogenous variables, i.e., (π1(δ, ε), π2(δ, ε)). All prices are
standardized (set to unity) at the reference state (δ, ε) = (1, 1), i.e., (π1(1, 1), π2(1, 1)) =
(1, 1). Moreover, we consider transcendental productivity changes in the same direction, i.e.,
(δ, ε) ≷ (1, 1), which correspond to (natural) productivity changes in the same direction, depending on the universal elasticity of substitution σ, as follows:

(δ, ε) = ((z1) [σ][−][1], (z2) [σ][−][1] ) ≷ (1, 1) ⇐⇒

(z1, z2) ≶ (1, 1) 1 − σ  - 0
(24)
(z1, z2) ≷ (1, 1) 1 − σ < 0

This document investigates how substitution elasticity affects the aggregation of sectoral productivity shocks within an economy's production network. The excerpt details the mathematical consequences of this elasticity, specifically showing how the relationship between simultaneous productivity changes and individual changes depends on the value of the universal elasticity of substitution $\sigma$. This leads to propositions defining negative sinergism for inelastic networks ($\sigma < 1$) and positive sinergism for elastic networks ($\sigma > 1$).\n\n11

The right hand sides are both positive as long as (δ, δ [′] ) ≷ (1, 1).
In any case,

π1(δ, ε) > π1(δ, 1)π1(1, ε) ⇐⇒ (p1(δ, ε)) [1][−][σ]   - (p1(δ, 1)p1(1, ε)) [1][−][σ]

π2(δ, ε) > π2(δ, 1)π2(1, ε) ⇐⇒ (p2(δ, ε)) [1][−][σ]   - (p2(δ, 1)p2(1, ε)) [1][−][σ]

Therefore, in regard to (25), for any (δ, ε) = ((z1) [σ][−][1], (z2) [σ][−][1] ) ≷ (1, 1), the price changes
are evaluated, depending on the universal elasticity of substitution, as follows:

log p1(δ, ε) ≷ log p1(δ, 1) + log p1(1, ε)

1 − σ ≷ 0
log p2(δ, ε) ≷ log p2(δ, 1) + log p2(1, ε)

Finally, the aggregate growths are evaluated as follows:

log V (δ, ε) ≶ log V (δ, 1) + log V (1, ε) 1 − σ ≷ 0 (27)

by applying (2), or specifically the followings:

log V (δ, ε) = −κ1 log p1(δ, ε) − κ2 log p2(δ, ε)

log V (δ, 1) = −κ1 log p1(δ, 1) − κ2 log p2(δ, 1)

log V (1, ε) = −κ1 log p1(1, ε) − κ2 log p2(1, ε)

Below we summarize the results obtained so far, concerning (27) with regard to (24):

Proposition 3 (Negative sinergism) . _Under an inelastic network (_ σ < 1 _), the aggregate out-_
_put pertaining to simultaneous productivity increases (or decreases), i.e.,_ (z1, z2) ≷ (1, 1) _,_
_is less than the sum of aggregate outputs that pertain to individual productivity increases (or_
_decreases), i.e.,_ (z1 ≷ 1, z2 = 1) _and_ (z1 = 1, z2 ≷ 1) _._

Proposition 4 (Positive sinergism) . _Under an elastic network (_ σ - 1 _), the aggregate output_
_pertaining_ _to simultaneous_ _productivity_ _increases (or decreases), i.e.,_ (z1, z2) ≷ (1, 1) _, is_
_greater than_ _the_ _sum_ _of aggregate_ _outputs_ _that_ _pertain to individual_ _productivity_ _increases_
_(or decreases), i.e.,_ (z1 ≷ 1, z2 = 1) _and_ (z1 = 1, z2 ≷ 1) _._

_(or decreases), i.e.,_ (z1 ≷ 1, z2 = 1) _and_ (z1 = 1, z2 ≷ 1) _._

4.2 Multisector model

Below is the multisector model (6) redisplayed in transcendent mode:

π1 = δ (α01 + α11π1 + α21π2 + α31π3 + · · · + αn1πn)

π2 = ε (α02 + α12π1 + α22π2 + α32π3 + · · · + αn2πn)

π3 = 1 (α03 + α13π1 + α23π2 + α33π3 + · · · + αn3πn)

... πn = 1 (α0n + α1nπ1 + α2nπ2 + α3nπ3 + · · · + αnnπn)

(28)

where, productivity changes are assumed to occur exclusively in the first and the second sectors. With obvious notational correspondence, we can rewrite the above system of equations
more concisely as follows:

              -               π1 = δ α01 + [π1, π2]A(1,2)1 + πrAr1 (29)

              -               π2 = ε α02 + [π1, π2]A(1,2)2 + πrAr2 (30)

              -               πr = I α0r + [π1, π2]A(1,2)r + πrArr (31)

This document investigates how substitution elasticities affect the aggregation of sectoral production in an economy-wide network. The excerpt details the mathematical derivation for a two-sector system and then analyzes the resulting equations for the remaining sectors. Specifically, it shows that under certain assumptions, the log of the production level for any sector exhibits superadditivity, implying a synergistic effect when productivity shocks occur across multiple sectors.\n\n12 Satoshi Nakano, Kazuhiko Nishimura

Here, A(1,2)1 and A(1,2)2 are 2×1 column vectors, πr and α0r are 1×(n−2) row vectors.
Ar1 and Ar2 are (n − 2) × 1 column vectors, A(1,2)r is a 2 × (n − 2) matrix, and Arr is
an (n − 2) × (n − 2) matrix. Notice the system of equations (31) can be solved as follows:

             -              πr = α0r + [π1, π2]A(1,2)r [I − Arr] [−][1] (32)

Plugging (32) into (29) and (30) yields the following:

     -     -     -     π1 = δ α01 + [π1, π2]A(1,2)1 + α0r + [π1, π2]A(1,2)r [I − Arr] [−][1] Ar1

     -     -     -     π2 = ε α02 + [π1, π2]A(1,2)2 + α0r + [π1, π2]A(1,2)r [I − Arr] [−][1] Ar2

With obvious notational correspondence, the system of equations (28) can be aggregated into
a two-sector system of equations equivalent to (23), as follows:

π1 = δ (ˆα01 + αˆ11π1 + αˆ21π2) = δ (˜α01 + α˜21π2)

π2 = ε (ˆα02 + αˆ12π1 + αˆ22π2) = ε (˜α02 + α˜12π1)

Thus, for the two sectors k = 1, 2, we know from (25) that for any (δ, ε) ≷ (1, 1),

log πk(δ, ε) ≥ log πk(δ, 1) + log πk(1, ε) (33)

As for the remaining sectors r = 3, · · ·, n, we redisplay one entry of (32) below, with
obvious notational correspondence:

πr = αˆ0r + αˆ1rπ1 + αˆ2rπ2 (34)

To proceed further, we make an assumption that transcendental productivity growths are
both infinitesimal, i.e., (log δ, log ε) ≈ (0, 0), so that πi(δ, ε) ≈ 1 for all i = 1, · · ·, n. The
assumption then allows us to apply π ≈ 1 + log π for (34), as follows:

1 + log πr = αˆ0r + αˆ1r(1 + log π1) + αˆ2r(1 + log π2)

But because αˆ0r + αˆ1r + αˆ2r = 1, this reduces to the following:

log πr = αˆ1r log π1 + αˆ2r log π2 (35)

By virtue of (33) and (35), we obtain the following:

log πr(δ, ε) = αˆ1r log π1(δ, ε) + αˆ2r log π2(δ, ε)

    - αˆ1r (log π1(δ, 1) + log π1(1, ε)) + αˆ2r (log π2(δ, 1) + log π2(1, ε))

= log πr(δ, 1) + log πr(1, ε) (36)

Hence, for any (log δ, log ε) ≷ (0, 0) that is infinitesimal, we know from (33) and (36) that:

log π(δ, ε) > log π(δ, 1) + log π(1, ε)

This document investigates how substitution elasticities affect the stability and aggregation of production networks, particularly when productivity shocks occur. The excerpt demonstrates a specific mathematical result showing that the logarithm of the general equilibrium price, $\log \pi$, exhibits a property of additivity when considering infinitesimal shocks, suggesting a form of synergism in the network's response.\n\nπr = αˆ1r log π1 + αˆ2r log π2 (35)

By virtue of (33) and (35), we obtain the following:

log πr(δ, ε) = αˆ1r log π1(δ, ε) + αˆ2r log π2(δ, ε)

    - αˆ1r (log π1(δ, 1) + log π1(1, ε)) + αˆ2r (log π2(δ, 1) + log π2(1, ε))

= log πr(δ, 1) + log πr(1, ε) (36)

Hence, for any (log δ, log ε) ≷ (0, 0) that is infinitesimal, we know from (33) and (36) that:

log π(δ, ε) > log π(δ, 1) + log π(1, ε) log p(δ, ε) ≷ log p(δ, 1) + log p(1, ε) 1 − σ ≷ 0

By applying (2), the presence of synergisms as noted in Propositions 3 and 4 are obvious as
we derive the following:

log V (δ, ε) = −(log p(δ, ε))κ

≶ −(log p(δ, 1))κ − (log p(1, ε))κ

= log V (δ, 1) + log V (1, ε) 1 − σ ≷ 0

This document explores the role of substitution elasticity in understanding network aggregation within economic models, particularly focusing on deviations from stable input-output linkages. The excerpt details the mathematical implications of relaxing an infinitesimal assumption, deriving a relationship involving the logarithm of the price function. This result suggests that the aggregation of infinitesimal changes in the price function is governed by a specific relationship involving the substitution elasticity parameter.\n\n13

Finally we relax the infinitesimal assumption by showing that integration is possible. By
utilizing not only (25) but also (26), we derive the following:

log π(δδ [′], εε [′] ) > log π(δδ [′], ε) + log π(δ, εε [′] ) > log π(δδ [′], 1) + log π(1, εε [′] )

        - log π(δ, 1) + log π(δ [′], 1) + log π(1, ε) + log π(1, ε [′] )

log p(δδ [′], εε [′] ) ≷ log p(δ, 1) + log p(δ [′], 1) + log p(1, ε) + log p(1, ε [′] ) 1 − σ ≷ 0

where (δ [′], ε [′] ) are infinitesimal changes. By applying (2) we are left with the following result
that allows us to integrate infinitesimal changes.

log V (δδ [′], εε [′] ) ≶ log V (δ, 1) + log V (δ [′], 1) + log V (1, ε) + log V (1, ε [′] ) 1 − σ ≷ 0

------------------------------------------------------------------------
This document investigates the role of substitution elasticity in determining the stability and potential singularities within an economy's production network, particularly when using CES production functions. The excerpt details a mathematical derivation involving logarithmic transformations of price functions, which is used to analyze the conditions under which the network's price structure exhibits singularity based on the substitution elasticity. This mathematical result supports the paper's broader discussion of how nonneutral substitution elasticities can lead to network singularities, contrasting this with other concepts of singularity like intelligence explosions.\n\n+ log π(1, ε [′] )

log p(δδ [′], εε [′] ) ≷ log p(δ, 1) + log p(δ [′], 1) + log p(1, ε) + log p(1, ε [′] ) 1 − σ ≷ 0

where (δ [′], ε [′] ) are infinitesimal changes. By applying (2) we are left with the following result
that allows us to integrate infinitesimal changes.

log V (δδ [′], εε [′] ) ≶ log V (δ, 1) + log V (δ [′], 1) + log V (1, ε) + log V (1, ε [′] ) 1 − σ ≷ 0

5 Concluding Remarks According to Eden et al (2012), singularity hypotheses refer to two distinct scenarios. One
is the intelligence explosion (Good, 1966), whereby machine intelligence surpasses human
intelligence through computational advancement, which entails recursive self-improvement.
The other scenario is the biointelligence explosion, which entails the amplification of human
capabilities to overcome all existing human limitations (Kurzweil, 2005). In both ways, however, the concept of singularity (i.e., explosion) does not go beyond exponential growth, if
not extreme. The economic singularity theory of Nordhaus (2021) also exploits the idea of
extreme exponential growth of intelligence capital deepening by a large elasticity of substitution. In contrast, the singularity presented in this study is based on matrix singularity where
the matrix is transformed by virtue of the substitution elasticity that is nonneutral.
Of course, positive singularity cannot take place unless some finite amount of non-labor
commodity can completely substitute the primary factor of production. Our model allowed
convergence towards positive singularity because our (standard) CES production function
allowed production even at the extreme position of the isoquant near the axes. On the other
hand, negative singularity is not an improbable event. With some productivity-diminishing
innovations, such as those involving environmental conservation technologies, network transformation under inelastic network can encounter a state that some commodity cannot be
produced in net. Finally, as regards the matter of industrial policy, whether to promote a
portfolio of projects, or rather to take a selective policy, depends on the synergism inherent
in the production network where the elasticity of substitution plays the important role.

By applying Roy’s identity to the Cobb-Douglas indirect utility function (1 right), we obtain the following:

Yuehao Bai

April 3, 2025 ### 1 Introduction
The past two decades have witnessed a surge of new research in the analysis of randomized experiments. The

emergence of this literature may seem surprising given the widespread use and long history of experiments

as the “gold standard” in program evaluation, but this body of work has revealed many subtle aspects of

randomized experiments that may have been previously unappreciated. This article provides an overview of

some of these topics, primarily focused on stratification, regression adjustment, and cluster randomization,

although we also provide short discussions on a broad range of other topics at the end of the article. We

also provide a companion R package `sreg`, which is designed to facilitate inference in stratified (as well as

potentially clustered) randomized experiments: see Trifonov et al. (2025).

The majority of our discussion is presented within the context of a framework in which units are sampled

from a suitable “super-population”; i.e., we assume that the units are i.i.d. draws from some probability dis

tribution. Importantly, these results differ from results that are derived within a complementary framework

∗We thank the co-editor and an anonymous referee for comments and suggestions that have improved the manuscript. We
would also like to thank Gustavo Bobonis for helpful discussions. The third author acknowledges support from NSF grant
SES-2149408.

This document provides a comprehensive review comparing two major frameworks for analyzing randomized experiments: design-based inference and super-population analysis. The excerpt fits into this context by immediately comparing these two sampling frameworks, specifically using the difference-in-means estimator for the average treatment effect as the primary illustration. It further details the relationship between the super-population and finite-population settings and introduces the topic of stratification as a subsequent area of focus.\n\nin which units are sampled from a fixed, finite population (without replacement). The special case in which

all the units from the finite population are sampled is sometimes referred to as _design-based_ inference. After

defining some common notation in Section 2, we begin our review in Section 3 by providing a comparison of

these two sampling frameworks. Specifically, we introduce both frameworks in the context of an analysis of

the difference-in-means estimator for the average treatment effect in a completely randomized experiment.

There, we argue that the super-population framework approximates a finite-population framework in which

a negligible fraction of the finite population is sampled; we argue further, however, that results derived in a

super-population framework may provide useful methods for inference in a finite population even outside of

this limiting case.

We then turn our attention in Section 4 to the impact of stratification on subsequent inferences drawn

from randomized experiments. More specifically, instead of assigning treatment status according to complete

randomization, it is common to stratify first according to some baseline covariates and then assign treatment

status within each stratum so as to ensure that the treatment group and control group in the experiment

are “balanced” according to these covariates. Examples of such schemes include stratified block randomiza

tion and matched pair designs, both of which are commonly used within economics and the sciences more

generally: see Rosenberger and Lachin (2015) for a textbook treatment focused on clinical trials and Bruhn

and McKenzie (2009) for a review focused on development economics. We first illustrate in Section 4.1 how

stratification can improve the precision of the usual difference-in-means estimator of the average treatment

effect by reducing what we refer to as the ex-post (as opposed to ex-ante) bias of the estimator. Here, the

This document provides a comprehensive primer on analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt details subsequent sections of the paper, outlining how the authors will discuss improving estimator precision through stratification and regression adjustment, and finally extending the analysis to cluster randomized experiments.\n\n(2015) for a textbook treatment focused on clinical trials and Bruhn

and McKenzie (2009) for a review focused on development economics. We first illustrate in Section 4.1 how

stratification can improve the precision of the usual difference-in-means estimator of the average treatment

effect by reducing what we refer to as the ex-post (as opposed to ex-ante) bias of the estimator. Here, the ex-post bias of the estimator is used to describe its behavior conditional on treatment status rather than

unconditionally. Section 4.2 develops the implications of this increase in precision for inferences about the

average treatment effect more formally for the special case of stratified block randomization with finitely

many “large” strata; Section 4.2.1 describes how this analysis changes when strata are “small,” for instance

in the case of matched pair designs.

In Section 5 we consider the use of baseline covariates (including ones that are possibly not used in

the assignment of treatment status) to further improve the precision of estimators of the average treatment

effect through regression adjustment. We explain how na¨ıve regression adjustment may increase rather than

decrease the precision of estimators of the average treatment effect, and how a more careful use of the

covariates can ensure an improvement in (asymptotic) precision. Our discussion includes linear adjustments

(Section 5.1) as well as more general forms of adjustment (Section 5.2).

Finally, Section 6 extends our discussion to cluster randomized experiments, i.e., randomized experiments

in which the unit of randomization is a cluster. Such designs are increasingly common in economics. Indeed,

Muralidharan and Niehaus (2017) find in a survey of leading economics journals between 2001 and 2016

that more than 75 percent of randomized experiments were cluster randomized. In Section 6.1 we draw

a distinction between different ways in which one may define the average effect of the treatment in such

settings. In particular, we discuss two possible parameters of interest, which we call the equally-weighted

This document provides a comprehensive review of analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt details the scope and limitations of the survey, specifically noting that while it focuses on analysis, it will only briefly touch upon experimental design and implementation issues.\n\naverage treatment effect and the size-weighted average treatment effect, that differ in how the average effect

within a cluster is aggregated across individuals. In Section 6.2 we discuss inference in cluster randomized

experiments.

Owing to space constraints, our discussion is, of course, necessarily incomplete. We therefore provide,

at the end of each section, a guide to some of the related literature on similar topics. For completeness, in

Section 7 we also briefly discuss a number of topics that were regrettably omitted from the main discussion:

this includes the analysis of treatment effect heterogeneity, re-randomization, multiple testing, imperfect

compliance/attrition, experiments with interference, randomization inference, policy learning, and response

adaptive designs. Athey and Imbens (2017) also provide a review of some of these topics (primarily from

the design based perspective).

Before proceeding, we emphasize that our survey is limited to the _analysis_ of randomized experiments,

and as a result we will only briefly comment on some aspects of experimental _design_ as well as practical

issues surrounding _implementation_, in passing; classical textbook treatments on experimental design are

provided in Cox and Reid (2000), Pukelsheim (2006), Atkinson et al. (2007), and Wu and Hamada (2011).

Other important contributions to the theory of experimental design (including theoretical justifications

for _why_ an experimenter may want to randomize) are provided in Savage (1951), Blackwell and Girshick

(1954), Kiefer (1959), Li (1983), Kallus (2018, 2021), Section 5.10 in Lehmann and Romano (2022), and Bai

(2023). Important references discussing other practical issues (particularly for field experiments conducted

in economics) include Duflo et al. (2007), Glennerster and Takavarasha (2013), Karlan and Appel (2017),

and List (2023).

This document provides a comprehensive primer on analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt introduces the necessary notation for this analysis, specifically defining potential outcomes for an individual based on whether they receive treatment or not. This section establishes the fundamental framework for understanding the outcomes in the experiments discussed throughout the paper.\n\nare provided in Savage (1951), Blackwell and Girshick

(1954), Kiefer (1959), Li (1983), Kallus (2018, 2021), Section 5.10 in Lehmann and Romano (2022), and Bai

(2023). Important references discussing other practical issues (particularly for field experiments conducted

in economics) include Duflo et al. (2007), Glennerster and Takavarasha (2013), Karlan and Appel (2017),

and List (2023). ### 2 Setup and Notation: The Potential Outcomes Framework
In this section, we present some notation which will be common to the majority of the article. Each individual

_i_ in the experiment is assigned a binary treatment _Di_ _∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ (we focus on settings with binary treatments,

but provide references on related extensions to multiple treatments throughout the article). Let _Yi_ (1) denote

the potential (or counterfactual) outcome for individual _i_ if they are treated, and _Yi_ (0) denote the potential

outcome if they are untreated. Note that we never observe _Yi_ (1) and _Yi_ (0) simultaneously for the same

individual, but rather we observe the outcome _Yi_ given by

_Yi_ =


 _Yi_ (1) if _Di_ = 1

 _Yi_ (0) if _Di_ = 0 _._

We summarize the previous relationship succinctly by

_Yi_ = _Yi_ (1) _Di_ + _Yi_ (0)(1 _−_ _Di_ ) _._

3

We emphasize that an important feature of our discussion here and throughout the rest of the article is that

we do not assume that the components of _D_ [(] _[n]_ [)] are independently distributed; this will play a crucial role in

our subsequent analyses when studying, for example, stratified randomization in Section 4. ### 3 What is Random? Finite versus Super-population Analyses of a Completely Randomized Experiment
In this section, we introduce the two main paradigms for the analysis of randomized experiments: the finite

population and super-population approaches to inference. In a finite-population analysis, we begin with a

collection _{_ ( _yj_ (1) _, yj_ (0) _, xj_ ) : 1 _≤_ _j_ _≤_ _N_ _}_ of _fixed_ quantities that constitute the entire population of interest.

A sample of size _n_ _≤_ _N_, given by _{_ ( _Yi_ (1) _, Yi_ (0) _, Xi_ ) : 1 _≤_ _i_ _≤_ _n}_, is then drawn _without_ replacement from

4

This document provides a primer on analyzing randomized experiments, comparing two main statistical frameworks: design-based inference and super-population analysis. The excerpt details the distinction between these two paradigms, explaining that design-based inference assumes sampling from a fixed population, while super-population analysis models data as independent and identically distributed. The authors highlight that the super-population view can yield consistent variance estimators, which contrasts with the finite-population framework where only conservative estimators are typically possible.\n\nthe population, and the experiment is performed on these _n_ individuals. The most common case considered

in the literature is when _n_ = _N_, so that the experiment is performed on the entire population. We will

refer to this case as _design-based_ inference, since the only source of uncertainty arises from the randomness

in _D_ [(] _[n]_ [)] . This perspective is often considered attractive in settings where it is difficult to conceptualize an

appropriate sampling frame; Reichardt and Gollob (1999) provide further discussion.

In contrast, in a super-population analysis, the sample _{_ ( _Yi_ (1) _, Yi_ (0) _, Xi_ ) : 1 _≤_ _i_ _≤_ _n}_ is modeled as

being i.i.d. according to some probability distribution. Informally, we may view the distribution from which

the potential outcomes are drawn as summarizing an essentially infinite “super-population.”

To compare and contrast the super- and finite population paradigms, we present an analysis of the

difference-in-means estimator ∆ [ˆ] _n_ for the average treatment effect under both approaches, following the orig

inal work of Neyman (1923), in a completely randomized experiment (as defined in Section 2). Our primary

takeaway will be that, in a sense to be made formal below, we can view the super-population paradigm as an

approximation to the finite-population paradigm in a regime where the sample size _n_ is a vanishing fraction

of the total population size _N_ . Consequently, we will show that in the super-population framework it is

possible to construct _consistent_ variance estimators of Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n_ ], whereas this will often be impossible in the

finite-population framework. Instead, in the finite population framework we will explain how to construct

_conservative_ variance estimators of Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n_ ]. Moreover, we will argue that consistent variance estimators

derived via a super-population analysis are often reasonable conservative variance estimators when viewed

3.2 Super-population analysis of ∆ [ˆ] _n_

Next, we repeat the above analysis within the super-population paradigm. Recall that in this case the

sample _{_ ( _Yi_ (1) _, Yi_ (0) _, Xi_ ) : 1 _≤_ _i ≤_ _n}_ is modeled as being i.i.d. according to some probability distribution.

Accordingly, in this case the average treatment effect is defined as

∆= _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) _−_ _Yi_ (0)] _,_

where the expectation is with respect to the distribution of the data.

We begin by documenting some finite-sample properties of the estimator ∆ [ˆ] _n_ in a completely randomized

experiment under the super-population framework, analogous to the properties derived in Section 3.1. Using

familiar properties of conditional expectations, we show in the appendix that

_E_ [ ∆ [ˆ] _n_ ] = _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) _−_ _Yi_ (0)] = ∆ _._ (5)

We thus obtain that the estimator ∆ [ˆ] _n_ is also an unbiased estimator of average treatment effect in the super

population paradigm. Following a similar line of reasoning using the properties of conditional variances, we

show in the appendix that

_[Y][i]_ [(1)]]

+ [Var[] _[Y][i]_ [(0)]]
_n_ 1 _n_ 0

Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n_ ] = [Var[] _[Y][i]_ [(1)]]

_n_ 0

It is instructive to compare the limits of the variance expressions in (2) and (6). To ensure nondegenerate

limits, it is useful to scale the variances by _n_ . By doing so, we see that the limit of the super-population

variance mirrors the limit of the finite-population variance in a regime where we sample a vanishing fraction

of the total population, i.e., _n/N_ _→_ 0. Since the variance of the unit-level treatment effects does not appear

in our expression for Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n_ ] in (6), consistent variance estimation will be feasible in the super-population

paradigm.

This document provides a comprehensive review of analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based inference (sampling from a finite population) with super-population analysis. The excerpt discusses the relationship between these two inference frameworks, noting that methods developed in the super-population setting often yield conservative results when applied to the finite population setting. This observation guides the remainder of the article to focus primarily on the super-population perspective while acknowledging differences when they appear.\n\nbe obtained from the resulting heteroskedasticity-robust variance estimator. An asymptotically valid 95%

confidence interval for ∆is therefore once again given by _Cn_ in (3). Moreover, since this variance estimator

is consistent, _Cn_ has _exact_ asymptotic coverage, that is

_P_ _{_ ∆ _∈_ _Cn} →_ 0 _._ 95 _,_

as _n →∞_ .

The above discussion illustrates an important feature of super-population and finite population analy

ses of randomized experiments: methods of inference developed in a super-population framework typically

_immediately_ deliver conservative methods of inference from a finite population perspective. While the pre

ceding discussion was limited to completely randomized experiments, in Appendix A.1, we show that this

feature holds much more generally under appropriate assumptions. With this in mind, the remainder of this

article will focus on illustrating some of the major themes in the analysis of randomized experiments from

the super-population perspective, but we will comment on any other important differences between these

two perspectives whenever they arise.

3.3 Further Reading

Most of the material in this section is by-now standard, and several textbook treatments exist at various

levels of formality: see in particular Imbens and Rubin (2015), Athey and Imbens (2017), and Lehmann

and Romano (2022). Li and Ding (2017) provide formal statements and proofs of finite population central

limit theorems. Harshaw et al. (2021) study a general method for constructing less conservative variance

estimators in the design-based paradigm.

This document provides a comprehensive review of analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt discusses stratified randomized experiments, explaining that stratification groups individuals by covariates to ensure sample balance between treatment and control groups. This technique can improve the precision of the difference-in-means estimator compared to simple complete randomization.\n\nseveral textbook treatments exist at various

levels of formality: see in particular Imbens and Rubin (2015), Athey and Imbens (2017), and Lehmann

and Romano (2022). Li and Ding (2017) provide formal statements and proofs of finite population central

limit theorems. Harshaw et al. (2021) study a general method for constructing less conservative variance

estimators in the design-based paradigm. ### 4 Stratified Randomized Experiments
In this section, we outline some benefits of _stratification_ and its consequences on subsequent experimental

analyses. In a stratified randomized experiment, individuals are first divided into groups (i.e., strata) sharing

similar values of their baseline covariates and then assigned to treatment so as to achieve “balance” across

the treatment and control groups: often, this amounts to simply performing complete randomization within

each stratum. Stratification is extremely common in the design of randomized experiments in all parts of the

sciences (some examples in economics include Duflo et al., 2015; Berry et al., 2018; Dizon-Ross, 2019; Callen

et al., 2020). A primary motivation for stratification, going back to the work of Fisher (1935), is to ensure

that the treatment and control groups are similar _in the sample_, in contrast to complete randomization which

can only ensure that this will be true _in_ _expectation_ . As we will show, this property of stratification can lead

to an increase in precision of the difference-in-means estimator ∆ [ˆ] _n_ relative to complete randomization, and

as a result subsequent inferences will be unnecessarily conservative unless this is taken into consideration.

9

sample _{_ ( _Yi_ (1) _, Yi_ (0) _, Xi_ ) : 1 _≤_ _i_ _≤_ _n}_, where for now we assume _Xi_ _∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ are binary variables, and for

1. Complete Randomization (CR): Treatment is completely randomized with _π_ = [1] 2 [(see] [Section] [2] [for] [a]

formal definition).

2. Stratified Block Randomization (SBR): Independently for each stratum (the sub-samples with _Xi_ = 0

and _Xi_ = 1), treatment is completely randomized with _π_ = [1] 2 [.]

Note that (SBR) as defined above uses the assignment proportion _π_ = [1] 2 [in] [both] [strata.] [Although] [main-]

taining a constant assignment proportion across all strata is the most common approach in practice, in

principle we could also consider using different assignment proportions in each stratum, and we comment

on the implications of doing so on subsequent analyses at the end of Section 4.2. Although both assignment

mechanisms (CR) and (SBR) assign exactly _n/_ 2 units to treatment, (CR) does not enforce that the _compo-_

_sition_ of the treated group across _Xi_ _∈{_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ matches the composition in the total sample. For instance,

suppose _n_ = 100, where 40 units have _Xi_ = 0 and 60 units have _Xi_ = 1. Figure 1 depicts one possible

assignment that could result from employing (CR) versus (SBR). Although (CR) guarantees that exactly 50

units are assigned to treatment, in this realization of the assignment, units with _X_ = 0 are over-represented

Bias [post] ( _X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] _, D_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ) _≡_ 0, whereas the ex-post bias under (CR) is not guaranteed to be identically zero unless

_Xi_ is an irrelevant stratification variable in the sense that _E_ [ _Yi_ (1)+ _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ = 1] = _E_ [ _Yi_ (1)+ _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ = 0].

Next, we show that these properties of the ex-post bias have direct implications for the (ex-ante) _variance_

of ∆ [ˆ] _n_ under (CR) and (SBR). By the law of total variance:

Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] = _E_ [Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] _, D_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] _|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] + Var[ _E_ [ ∆ [ˆ] _n|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] _, D_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] _|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] _._

We show in the appendix that

(Var[ _Yi_ (1) _|Xi_ ] + Var[ _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ ]) _._ (8)

1 _≤i≤n_

In words, comparing Var[ ∆ [ˆ] _n|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] under (CR) versus (SBR) amounts to a comparison of the second moment

of the ex-post bias under both designs; because the ex-post bias is always zero under (SBR), the variance

of ∆ [ˆ] _n_ is always smaller under (SBR) than (CR). In the next section, we discuss the implications of this

increase in precision on subsequent inferences.

4.2.1 Variance Estimation with Small Strata

When _X_ was discrete, a natural estimator of _V_ [sbr] could be constructed by computing sample analogs of the

means and variances of the potential outcomes at the stratum level. If, however, _Xi_ contains continuous

4.2.1 Variance Estimation with Small Strata

When _X_ was discrete, a natural estimator of _V_ [sbr] could be constructed by computing sample analogs of the

means and variances of the potential outcomes at the stratum level. If, however, _Xi_ contains continuous

components and the experiment is “finely stratified” in such a way that there is only one treated or control observation per stratum, then this logic breaks down. A leading example of when this occurs is with pair

wise matching: in a matched pairs experiment, units are paired together based on their observed covariate

values and then treatment is assigned such that, in each pair, one unit is selected at random to receive

treatment and the other control. With only one observation per stratum with a given treatment, we cannot

estimate the stratum-level variances by simply taking sample analogs as proposed in the previous section. As

discussed in Klar and Donner (1997), this added challenge to variance estimation has often been perceived

as a fundamental analytical limitation of matched pair designs. In this section, we briefly illustrate how the

variance of ∆ [ˆ] _n_ can still be consistently estimated by using a “collapsed-strata” estimator in the spirit of

Hansen et al. (1953).

To streamline the exposition, in what follows we focus only on the setting where _π_ = 21 [and] [units]

are matched into pairs. First, note that it can be shown under appropriate assumptions that, even with

small strata, the limiting variance of ∆ [ˆ] _n_ is still given by (9) (see, for instance, Bai et al., 2022). The

unconditional variances Var[ _Yi_ (1)] and Var[ _Yi_ (0)] in (9) are consistently estimable using their sample coun

14

We thus focus on estimating the last term on the right-hand side of (9), which in this case is

(1 _/_ 2) Var[ _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) + _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ ]]. To that end, note that by elementary properties of the variance and the law

of iterated expectations,

Var[ _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) + _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ ]] = _E_ [ _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) + _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ ] [2] ] _−_ _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) + _Yi_ (0)] [2] _._

The second term on the right-hand side is again unconditional and thus easy to estimate using a sample

analog. Therefore, it suffices to consistently estimate

_E_ [ _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) + _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ ] [2] ] _._ (12)

Intuitively, to estimate this last quantity we would want independent variation in _Yi_ (1) and _Yi_ (0) for each

given value of _Xi_ . Let us suppose for a moment that we did in fact have two pairs of units _{Y_ 1 _, Y_ 2 _}_ and

_{Y_ 3 _, Y_ 4 _}_ sharing the same value of _X_ . In each pair, there is exactly one treated and control observation per

stratum. It follows that that _Y_ 1 + _Y_ 2 and _Y_ 3 + _Y_ 4 both share the same conditional mean _E_ [ _Y_ (1) + _Y_ (0) _|X_ ].

Moreover, across pairs, the outcomes are (conditionally) independent. As a consequence, we might conjecture

that ( _Y_ 1 + _Y_ 2)( _Y_ 3 + _Y_ 4) is approximately equal to _E_ [ _Y_ (1)+ _Y_ (0) _|X_ ] [2] +( _ϵ_ 1 + _ϵ_ 2)( _ϵ_ 3 + _ϵ_ 4), where _E_ [ _ϵiϵj|X_ [(] _[n]_ [)] ] = 0

for _i_ and _j_ in different pairs. Therefore, we can construct a consistent estimator for (12) by averaging over

these products across pairs of pairs. Of course in practice, this scenario is not realistic, as units which are

paired together will typically not share the same value of _X_ . If, however, matching is performed so that we

expect pairs and “adjacent” pairs to have similar characteristics, then this intuition can be formalized to

construct a consistent variance estimator for (12), and thus it is possible to construct a consistent estimator

This document provides a comprehensive review of analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt discusses advanced topics related to variance estimation and inference, specifically referencing matched-pair designs and citing recent literature that builds upon the foundational concepts presented in the main body of the work.\n\nin practice, this scenario is not realistic, as units which are

paired together will typically not share the same value of _X_ . If, however, matching is performed so that we

expect pairs and “adjacent” pairs to have similar characteristics, then this intuition can be formalized to

construct a consistent variance estimator for (12), and thus it is possible to construct a consistent estimator of _V_ [sbr] even with “small” strata; for a formal exposition of this idea, see Bai et al. (2022, 2024e).

4.3 Further Reading

The exposition in Section 4.1 is most closely inspired by Bai (2022), who establishes the finite-sample

optimality of certain matched-pair designs. Much of the discussion in Section 4.2 comes from Bugni et al.

(2018), who study inference for covariate-adaptive randomization when the treated fraction is constant across

strata; Bugni et al. (2019) extends the analysis to settings with multiple treatments and where the treatment

proportions are allowed to differ across strata. Bai et al. (2022) studies inference for matched pair designs

and develops the estimation strategy discussed in Section 4.2.1. Cytrynbaum (2023b) generalizes these

estimation and inference procedures beyond pair-matching and jointly analyzes the stratification problem

combined with the problem of selecting a representative sample based on covariates. Bai et al. (2024e)

generalizes Bai et al. (2022) to settings with multiple treatments. Pashley and Miratrix (2021) and Bai et al.

(2025a) provide overviews of design-based analyses of stratified experiments.

This document provides a comprehensive overview of analyzing randomized experiments, contrasting design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt focuses specifically on the technique of regression adjustment within randomized experiments, discussing its motivation, potential pitfalls, and more robust estimation strategies.\n\n### 5 Regression Adjustment in Randomized Experiments
In this section, we consider the role of regression adjustment using baseline covariates in the analysis of

randomized experiments. A primary motivation for regression adjustment is that it hopefully improves

estimation precision in settings where the covariates are correlated with the experimental outcome. This

practice has, however, often come under scrutiny. An influential paper of Freedman (2008), for instance,

points out that standard linear regression adjustment only guarantees a gain in precision under strong

assumptions, and concludes that in general “[...] randomization does not justify the assumptions behind

the OLS model.” In Section 5.1 we review part of Freedman’s critique, present a resolution popularized

by Lin (2013), and briefly discuss some implications for inference. In Section 5.2 we explain how Lin’s

resolution is a special case of an estimation procedure based on the doubly-robust moment condition of

the average treatment effect, and show how this perspective leads to more general regression adjustment

strategies beyond linear adjustment.

5.1 Linear Regression Adjustment in a Completely Randomized Experiment

We revisit the setting of a completely randomized experiment with _π_ _∈_ (0 _,_ 1). In particular, we assume

that treatment assignment is independent of the outcomes _and_ the baseline covariates; we briefly comment

on settings where the treatment assignment may itself depend on the baseline covariates (for instance, in a

stratified randomized experiment) in Section 5.1.1 and provide further references in Section 5.3. To begin,

recall from (1) that the unadjusted difference-in-means estimator can be described as the OLS estimator of

the coefficient on _Di_ in a linear regression of _Yi_ on a constant and _Di_ . A natural starting point for regression

adjustment is then to instead consider the OLS estimator of the coefficient on _Di_ in a linear regression of _Yi_

It then transitions to reviewing the classical problem of estimating a population mean using regression, setting up a comparison between different regression models.\n\nwhere _γ_ denotes the probability limit of _γ_ ˜ _n_, Σ _X_ denotes the covariance matrix of _Xi_, and _γ_ ( _d_ ) is the

or _γ_ (1) = _γ_ (0), so that in these cases we can in fact conclude that _V_ [pool] _≤_ _V_ [cr] . In words, regression

adjustment based on the regression in (13) is not guaranteed to improve precision in general, but (weakly)

improves precision in the special cases where assignment is equal between treatment and control, or treatment

effects are sufficiently homogeneous, e.g., if treatment effects are constant such that _Yi_ (1) _−_ _Yi_ (0) = ∆.

The difficulty with estimation based on (13) is that the regression pools together the observations under

both treatment and control when estimating the relationship between the outcome and the covariates. To

explain how this could be resolved, we first review the classical problem of estimation of a population mean

5.1.1 Inference on ∆ when using Linear Regression Adjustment: Some Caveats

We briefly discuss some complications surrounding inference on ∆based on the regressions (13) and (16)

under complete randomization, and mention some related complications when generalizing beyond complete

randomization. Recall that in Section 3 we explained that the robust variance estimator obtained from the

regression in (1) is consistent for the asymptotic variance of the difference-in-means estimator ∆ [ˆ] _n_ . A natural

follow-up question is then whether or not the robust variance estimators obtained from the regressions (13)

and (16) are consistent for (or at least an upper bound on) the asymptotic variances of _β_ [˜] _n_ and _β_ [ˆ] _n_, respectively.

In the case of the regression in (13), the answer is yes: the robust variance estimator is consistent because _β_ [˜] _n_

is simply the OLS estimator of the best linear predictor of _Y_ given 1 _, D,_ and _X_ . In the case of the regression

in (16), however, the answer is _no_ whenever we view the sample as being drawn from a larger (finite or super-)

population. The issue here is similar to our discussion surrounding consistent estimators of the variance when

studying stratified randomized experiments in Section 4.2. In particular, note that the regression in (16)

design-based framework (in which case _X_ [¯] _n_ is non-random). When moving beyond complete randomization

to settings with stratified assignment, the complications become more subtle. When stratification is based on

a finite number of discrete categories, an easy solution is to perform regression adjustment in each stratum

separately and then aggregate the stratum-level estimates; we provide relevant references for this and more

complicated settings involving stratification in Section 5.3. With these considerations in mind, we suggest

that practitioners use our R command `sreg` to perform regression adjustment in (stratified) randomized

experiments: see Trifonov et al. (2025).

5.2 Double Robustness and General Regression Adjustments

In this section, we explain how Lin’s interacted regression (16) is a special case of a more general class of

estimators based on a _doubly-robust_ moment function. To simplify the exposition we once again assume

that treatment is completely randomized with treated fraction _π_ _∈_ (0 _,_ 1). Note that because the treatments

are independent of the potential outcomes and covariates, the probability of assignment of any individual _i_

satisfies _P_ _{Di_ = 1 _|Xi_ = _x}_ _≡_ _π_ . Moreover, it follows that the treatments are independent of the potential

outcomes _conditional_ on the covariates.

moment equation for the ATE due to Robins et al. (1995). [2] Since _π_ is known in a randomized experiment

2The term “doubly-robust” is due to the fact that, if conversely _µ_ ˜1( _x_ ) = _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) _|Xi_ = _x_ ] and _µ_ ˜0( _x_ ) = _E_ [ _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ = _x_ ],
equation (18) still identifies ∆even if _π_ = _P_ _{Di_ = 1 _|Xi_ = _x}_ .

estimator of the average treatment effect. Under appropriate assumptions, it can be shown that ∆ [ˆ] [AIPW] _n_ is

_V_ _[∗]_ = _E_ - Var[ _Yi_ (1) _|Xi_ ]

1 _−_ _π_ + Var[ _E_ [ _Yi_ (1) _−_ _Yi_ (0) _|Xi_ ]] _,_

see Tu et al. (2023), Rafi (2023) for details. Importantly, this variance is the efficient variance for estimating

∆using a randomized experiment when the probability of assignment is exogenously constrained to be _π_

(see Armstrong, 2022; Rafi, 2023; Bai et al., 2025b).

Note that _V_ _[∗]_ coincides exactly with the asymptotic variance _V_ [sbr] obtained when using the unadjusted

estimator ∆ [ˆ] _n_ in a finely stratified randomized experiment with “small” strata (see Section 4.2.1). We have

thus demonstrated two alternative methods for achieving the efficient variance _V_ _[∗]_ when estimating ∆via a

randomized experiment: [3]

1. Assign treatment using complete randomization and estimate ∆using ∆ [ˆ] [AIPW] _n_ with suitable non

parametric estimators _µ_ ˆ _d,n_ ( _·_ ) of the conditional means _E_ [ _Yi_ ( _d_ ) _|Xi_ ].

2. Assign treatment using “fine stratification” (for instance, if _π_ = 1 _/_ 2 this could be a matched pairs

design as described in Section 4.2.1) and estimate ∆using ∆ [ˆ] _n_ .

3Of course, this list of methods is not exhaustive. For instance, efficient estimation could also be achieved under i.i.d.
randomization using a standard inverse-propensity weighted (IPW) estimator, where the propensity score is _non-parametrically_
estimated as a function of _X_ : see in particular Hirano et al. (2003) for details.

This document provides a comprehensive primer on analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt discusses advanced topics related to regression adjustment in randomized experiments, specifically referencing how fine stratification can mimic non-parametric regression adjustment and pointing to recent literature on combining stratification with regression adjustment.\n\nThis demonstrates that experiments which assign treatment using fine stratification effectively perform non

parametric regression adjustment “ _by_ _design_ ”; this feature of finely stratified experiments is further explored

in Cytrynbaum (2023b) and Bai et al. (2025b). In principle, both strategies could be combined, by first

stratifying on the covariates which might be expected to be most predictive for the outcome of interest, and

then adjusting for a (potentially high-dimensional) set of additional covariates via regression ex-post: see

Bai et al. (2024c) for details.

5.3 Further Reading

The exposition in Section 5.1 closely follows Negi and Wooldridge (2021) and Ma et al. (2022), although

some of their expressions have been modified so that they could be more easily related to Sections 3 and

4 of this paper. The use of doubly-robust estimators for regression adjustment in randomized experiments

goes back at least to the work of Tsiatis et al. (2008); Tu et al. (2023) and Rafi (2023) extend these results

to general stratified experiments with finitely many strata. Bai et al. (2024c) and Cytrynbaum (2023a)

consider regression adjustment in settings with “small” strata, in the sense of Section 4.2.1. Wang et al.

(2023a) study regression adjustment for general parameters defined by estimating equations. Recent work

on design-based analyses of regression adjusted estimators can be found in Aronow and Middleton (2013),

Wu and Gagnon-Bartsch (2018), Liu et al. (2021), Chang (2023), Chiang et al. (2023).

meta.source: 2405.03910v2.pdf

------------------------------------------------------------------------
This document provides a comprehensive review of analyzing randomized experiments, contrasting design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt specifically introduces the analysis of cluster randomized experiments, which differ from the previously discussed individual-level treatment assignments. It explains that cluster assignment occurs when treatment is applied to a group, such as a school, affecting all members within that group.\n\nregression adjustment in settings with “small” strata, in the sense of Section 4.2.1. Wang et al.

(2023a) study regression adjustment for general parameters defined by estimating equations. Recent work

on design-based analyses of regression adjusted estimators can be found in Aronow and Middleton (2013),

Wu and Gagnon-Bartsch (2018), Liu et al. (2021), Chang (2023), Chiang et al. (2023). ### 6 The Analysis of Cluster Randomized Experiments
Until now, our focus has been on experiments where treatment is assigned at the individual level and

each individual’s outcome depends only on their own treatment. In this section, we consider randomized

experiments where treatment is assigned at an aggregated level which we call a _cluster_ : for example, when

evaluating an educational intervention we may observe outcomes at the student-level, but assign treatment

at the school-level, so that every student in the school receives the same treatment. This type of cluster-level

assignment is extremely common in practice: see, for instance, Angrist and Lavy (2009); Banerjee et al.

(2015); Cr´epon et al. (2015); Bruhn et al. (2016); Romero et al. (2020). There are two common explanations

for why a researcher would consider cluster-level assignment as opposed to individual-level assignment: first,

there could be logistical constraints on the experiment that require that every individual in a cluster receives

the same treatment. Second, we may be concerned that there is treatment _interference_, i.e., the treatment

statuses of individuals in a cluster may affect the outcomes of others. In Section 6.1 we introduce the

framework and define some relevant analogs to the average treatment effect in this setting. In Section 6.2,

we discuss inference in cluster randomized experiments under complete randomization and stratified block

randomization.

21

This document provides a comprehensive primer on analyzing randomized experiments, contrasting design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt details the necessary notational adjustments for analyzing cluster randomized experiments, introducing parameters that generalize the average treatment effect by considering how individual treatment effects are aggregated across clusters.\n\n6.1 Defining Average Treatment Effects in Cluster Randomized Experiments

To accommodate cluster-level assignment, we first modify our notation relative to what we have considered

thus far. Let _Yi,g_ denote the observed outcome of the _i_ th unit in the _g_ th cluster, _Dg_ denote an indicator

for whether or not the _g_ th cluster is treated, and _Ng_ the size of the _g_ th cluster. Further denote by _Yi,g_ (1)

the potential outcome of the _i_ th unit in the _g_ th cluster if the cluster is treated and by _Yi,g_ (0) the potential

outcome of the _i_ th unit in the _g_ th cluster if not treated. As usual, the observed outcome and potential

outcomes are related to treatment assignment by the relationship

_Yi,g_ = _Yi,g_ (1) _Dg_ + _Yi,g_ (0)(1 _−_ _Dg_ ) _._

In practice it is sometimes the case that the researcher does not sample all of the units in a given cluster.

To allow for this possibility, define _Mg_ to be the subset of _{_ 1 _, . . ., Ng}_ corresponding to the observations

within the _g_ th cluster that are sampled by the researcher. For example, in the event that all observations in

a cluster are sampled, _Mg_ = _{_ 1 _, . . ., Ng}_ and _|Mg|_ = _Ng_ .

With this updated notation, our sampling framework models

_{_ (( _Yi,g_ (1) _, Yi,g_ (0) : 1 _≤_ _i ≤_ _Ng_ ) _, Mg, Ng_ ) : 1 _≤_ _g_ _≤_ _G}_ _,_

as a collection of _G_ independent and identically distributed draws from a distribution of clusters. Importantly,

we note here that the cluster sizes _Ng_ are modeled as random variables which are potentially related to the

potential outcomes. In this framework, we introduce two natural parameters that arise as generalizations of

the average treatment effect ∆which we focused on in earlier sections. These parameters differ in the way

they aggregate, or average, the individual level treatment effects.

Both parameters of interest we consider can be written as weighted averages of the cluster-level average

treatment effects:

_Ng_

This document provides a comprehensive review of analyzing randomized experiments, contrasting design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt focuses specifically on cluster randomized experiments, introducing and comparing two key estimators for the average treatment effect: $\Delta_{eq}$ and $\Delta_{size}$. It explains that these two parameters are distinct when both treatment effect heterogeneity and cluster-size heterogeneity are present, guiding the reader on which measure is relevant depending on the policy question.\n\nsize:

( _Yi,g_ (1) _−_ _Yi,g_ (0))



∆ [size] := _E_



1
 _E_ [ _Ng_ ]

 _._

We refer to this quantity as the size-weighted cluster-level average treatment effect. Since ∆ [size] assigns

a weight proportional to each cluster’s size, it can be thought of as the average treatment effect where

individuals are the units of interest.

Note that in empirical settings with treatment effect heterogeneity (so that _Yi,g_ (1) _−_ _Yi,g_ (0) is random)

and cluster-size heterogeneity, we should expect that ∆ [eq] and ∆ [size] are indeed distinct parameters with

differing policy interpretations. For instance, suppose the experiment studies the effect of an educational

intervention on students’ reading level. If the policy-maker is interested in raising the average reading level

across all students, then the magnitude of ∆ [size] is the relevant parameter. If, on the other hand, the policy

maker has concerns about raising the average reading level across all schools, then the magnitude of ∆ [eq]

would also be important to consider.

6.2 Inference in Cluster Randomized Experiments

In this section, we study estimation and inference on ∆ [eq] and ∆ [size] . We begin with the setting of a

completely randomized experiment; note that in the context of a cluster randomized experiment this means

that a fraction _π_ _∈_ (0 _,_ 1) of _clusters_ is assigned to treatment and the rest to control.

6.3 Further Reading

The material in this section is most closely related to Bugni et al. (2022) and Bai et al. (2024d). Donner

and Klar (2000) contains a textbook treatment of early work in cluster randomized experiments. Su and

Ding (2021) and Wang et al. (2024) study regression adjustment for cluster randomized experiments without

stratification (Bugni et al. (2022) and Bai et al. (2024d) discuss extensions of these results to settings

with stratification). Other recent work on cluster randomized experiments (mostly from a design-based

perspective) includes Imai et al. (2009), Schochet (2013), Middleton and Aronow (2015), Schochet et al.

(2021), de Chaisemartin and Ramirez-Cuellar (2024).

meta.source: 2405.03910v2.pdf

This document provides a comprehensive primer on analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt details advanced topics within this field, specifically discussing heterogeneity in treatment effects and the technique of re-randomization. This section serves to guide the reader toward more specialized areas of research beyond the core comparisons presented earlier in the paper.\n\n### 7 Other Topics
7.1 Treatment Effect Heterogeneity and Quantile Treatment Effects

This article focused exclusively on estimation and inference of the unconditional average treatment effect.

Recent work on the analysis of randomized experiments has studied inference for quantile treatment effects:

see in particular Zhang and Zheng (2020), Jiang et al. (2021), and Jiang et al. (2023). We may also be

interested in heterogeneity of the average treatment effect as a function of the observable characteristics

(i.e., the conditional average treatment effect, or CATE). Although estimation and inference on the CATE

has been an extremely active area of research in causal inference more broadly (see, for instance, Kennedy,

2023, for an in-depth discussion and further references), to our knowledge almost all of this work maintains

the assumption that treatment assignment is independent and identically distributed across individuals; this

precludes, for instance, stratified block randomization or pair matching. Some exceptions are Zhang and

Ma (2023), who present tests for treatment-covariate interactions and study their properties under general

covariate-adaptive stratified randomization schemes, and Ding et al. (2019); Cytrynbaum (2025), who study

parametric approximations to the CATE under complete randomization and finely stratified re-randomization

designs, respectively.

7.2 Re-randomization

Re-randomization is a method of experimental assignment which, in a similar spirit to stratification, is

intended to enforce “balance” in the covariate distributions between treatment and control groups. In a

standard re-randomization procedure, researchers specify a balance criterion for the covariate values and

then repeatedly generate assignments using a completely randomized design until an assignment is found

which achieves an acceptable covariate distribution according to the balance criterion. An excellent historical

This document provides a comprehensive primer on analyzing randomized experiments, comparing design-based and super-population inference frameworks. The excerpt discusses advanced topics related to experimental design, specifically focusing on re-randomization procedures used to balance covariates between treatment and control groups. It cautions that while re-randomization increases precision, the resulting inferences require careful correction because the limiting distribution of the difference-in-means estimator may not be asymptotically normal.\n\nin the covariate distributions between treatment and control groups. In a

standard re-randomization procedure, researchers specify a balance criterion for the covariate values and

then repeatedly generate assignments using a completely randomized design until an assignment is found

which achieves an acceptable covariate distribution according to the balance criterion. An excellent historical ### 7 Other Topics
summary is given in Morgan and Rubin (2012). Similarly to stratification, re-randomization leads to an

increase in precision relative to complete randomization, and as a result inferences will be unnecessarily

conservative unless this is taken into consideration. However, unlike with stratification, where correcting this

issue usually amounts to simply modifying the standard errors, corrected inferences for re-randomization are

sometimes further complicated by the fact that the limiting distribution of the difference-in-means estimator

is not asymptotically normal (although normality can be restored using ex-post linear adjustment: see Li

and Ding, 2020; Cytrynbaum, 2025, for details). For an in-depth theoretical discussion of re-randomization,

see Li et al. (2018), Li et al. (2020), Zhao and Ding (2021b), Lu et al. (2023), Branson et al. (2024). Wang

et al. (2023b) and Cytrynbaum (2025) study how to combine re-randomization with stratification.
