This document investigates the phase transition characteristics within the context of a Higgs-confinement crossover using a U(1) gauge U(1) global model. The excerpt details lattice simulation results concerning the magnetic flux correlation function, demonstrating long-range order associated with a vortex. This observation suggests that the phase transition between confinement and Higgs regimes is not evident through bulk observables, contrasting with ordinary symmetry-breaking phases.\n\n### _l_
Figure 2 . Correlation function of the magnetic flux Γ. The correlation function approaches nonzero
values at a long distance above a critical coupling constant, indicating the long-range order that is
observed only in the presence of a vortex.

Similarly, we can define the magnetic flux and its correlation function for the anti-vortex
located at ( _x_ 1 _, x_ 2) = ( _N_ 1 1 _/_ 2 _,_ ( _N_ 2 1) _/_ 2). We compute the correlation function both for
_−_ _−_
vortex and anti-vortex, and show the average of the results in figure 2. The lattice volume
is _V_ = 16 [4] . We take _βH_ = 2 _._ 0 and change _βg_ from strong to weak coupling. Under these
parameters, we do not observe the phase transition between the confinement and Higgs regime,

i.e., no phase transition is detected in the lens of bulk observables. As clearly seen in figure 2,
we observe the long-range order of Γ( _x_ 3 _, x_ 4), i.e., the correlation approaches nonzero values
at long distance above a critical coupling constant. Such a long-range order is measurable

only in the presence of a vortex, which is in stark contrast to the ordinary phases of matters

characterized by spontaneous symmetry breaking.

To quantify the properties of the phase transition such as its order, and if it is second order,

to determine its universality class, we performed the finite-size scaling analysis. Following the

scenario proposed by Motrunich and Senthil [41], we assume the Ising universality class for

the finite-size scaling analysis. To this end, we define the susceptibility _χ_ from fluctuations of

                   - 13

This document investigates the phase transition characteristics within the Higgs-confinement crossover using a U(1)gauge U(1)global model. The excerpt summarizes the paper's main contribution: proposing a novel classification method for distinguishing states of matter, even when the bulk system undergoes a smooth crossover, by examining spontaneous symmetry breaking on extended objects like vortices. Specifically, the authors demonstrate that the two states in their model can be distinguished by observing a phase transition on a vortex, even if bulk observables suggest a crossover.\n\n### ( βg βc ) L _−_
Figure 5 . Renormalized susceptibility _χL_ _[−][γ/ν]_ as a function of the renormalized coupling constant
( _βg_ _βc_ ) [1] _[/ν]_ for _βH_ = 2 _._ 0, and _V_ = _L_ [4] = 24 [4], 28 [4] and 32 [4] ( _V_ = 16 [4], 20 [4] are also shown just for

All data collapse into the single curve implies that the observed phase transition belongs to the Ising
universality class.

4 Summary

We have proposed a novel classification method to distinguish states of matter in the case

that the bulk system changes smoothly without a phase transition based on spontaneous

symmetry breaking in extended objects such as topological defects. As an example, we have
studied the phase transition on a vortex in a U(1)gauge U(1)global lattice model in (3 + 1)
_×_
dimension, where the effective theory is a (1 + 1)-dimensional field theory with Z2 global
symmetry. Even though a crossover is expected in terms of bulk observables, the two states

may still be distinguishable through a vortex as a topological defect, and by observing a phase

transition on it. In other words, the two states can be classified by spontaneous symmetry
breaking of Z2 global symmetry on a votrtex. What kind of topological defects one can use
as a probe depends on the symmetry-breaking pattern of the bulk state and can be classified

using a conventional method. It is important to construct the low-energy effective theory of

topological defect to discuss the possible state based on the phase transition in a topological

                   - 16

FRAGMENTO 812 / 824
tipo_contenido: Texto (Markdown / pymupdf4llm)
tipo (clave): texto_markdown
id: 2411.03676v2.pdf_pag18_chunk0
meta.chunk: 0
meta.format: markdown
meta.page: 18
meta.section_header: 
meta.source: 2411.03676v2.pdf
meta.total_chunks_in_page: 2
------------------------------------------------------------------------
This document investigates phase transitions occurring on superfluid vortices within the context of the Higgs-Confinement crossover in a U(1) gauge U(1) global model. The excerpt discusses the limitations of applying the model's findings to other physical systems, such as the CFL vortex in finite-density QCD, and provides a concrete example using the quantum Ising model to illustrate the symmetry breaking criteria discussed in the paper.\n\ndefect.

We have considered a simple vortex; in general, in addition to extended objects like domain

walls and vortices, we can consider junctions of domain walls, where a phase transition may

also occur. It is also possible to consider line-like objects, such as Wilson loops, in which

case the effective theory becomes a (0+1)-dimensional field theory, i.e., quantum mechanics.

Although a strict phase transition does not occur in quantum mechanics, phenomena similar

to level crossing may occur.

Regarding the hadron-quark continuity in finite-density QCD, our results are not directly
applicable since the effective theory of CFL vortex is C _P_ [2] model according to the symmetry

breaking pattern [40, 43–45], which has no discrete symmetry, and spontaneous symmetry

breaking is not allowed by Coleman-Mermin-Wagner theorem [46, 47]. On the other hand,

gapless majorana fermions exist on a CFL vortex [48], and thus the low-energy effective theory
of CFL vortex may not be the simple C _P_ [2] model [49]. There is still a possibility that the
interaction with fermions changes the phase structure of the C _P_ [2] model. We will study it in

future works.

Acknowledgements

Y.H. thanks Yuya Tanizaki, Yui Hayashi, and Eiji Muto for their valuable discussions. A

part of the numerical simulations has been performed using cluster computers at iTHEMS

in RIKEN, and Yukawa-21 at YITP in Kyoto University. This work was supported by JSPS

KAKENHI Grant Numbers JP21H01084, JP24H00975, JP24K00630 and JP24KJ0613.

whose Hamiltonian is given by

This example illustrates the theoretical concepts discussed in the paper by showing a model with an ordered $\mathbb{Z}_2$ symmetry breaking phase and a disordered phase.\n\nJP21H01084, JP24H00975, JP24K00630 and JP24KJ0613.
